title: Treating active woodworm
subtitle: Identify live infestation, apply treatment, monitor for re-emergence
excerpt: How to treat active woodworm in furniture and structural timber: distinguishing live from old infestation by checking for fresh frass, applying a boron-based or permethrin woodworm treatment, and confirming eradication at the next flight season.
sourceNotes: HSE guidance on woodworm treatment. English Heritage conservation practice note on furniture beetle control. Product data from Permagard and Rentokil woodworm treatment ranges. PCA (Property Care Association) guidance on Anobium punctatum.

content.text: Most
woodworm

found in second-hand furniture and old structural timber is inactive. Treating an old infestation is unnecessary; the holes are cosmetic. Treatment is only needed when larvae are still alive and boring, which the presence of fresh
frass
confirms.
Identifying live infestation
Step 1, check for fresh frass.

Slide a sheet of white paper under the suspect piece. Tap the timber firmly. If fine pale-yellow powder falls onto the paper, the infestation is active; larvae are still boring and pushing frass out. If the powder is dark or grey, the holes are old. Run this test in late spring and early summer, when adult beetles are most active and new exit holes appear.
Step 2, assess the extent of damage.

Press along the surface of the timber with a thumb. Sound timber feels hard; heavily tunnelled timber feels spongy and may crush. If a structural beam or joist is compromised, a structural survey is needed before treatment. Woodworm treatment kills larvae; it does not restore lost timber strength.
Applying treatment
Step 3, prepare the area.

Woodworm treatment fluid contains either permethrin or a boron compound as the active ingredient. Both are toxic to insects and require the same precautions: wear nitrile gloves and eye protection. Remove or protect nearby textiles and food surfaces. Ventilate the room throughout and for 30 minutes after application.
Step 4, apply the treatment.

Brush or spray the treatment fluid generously onto all bare, unpolished timber surfaces: tops, undersides, and sides of the affected piece. Pay particular attention to the joints, backs, and underside where the timber is unfinished. The fluid must penetrate the surface; it does not soak through a varnish or wax finish. Apply two coats if using a low-concentration product, following the manufacturer's drying time between coats.
Step 5, allow to dry fully.

Leave the treated piece in a ventilated space for the full drying time specified by the manufacturer, typically 24 to 48 hours. Do not use the furniture until it is dry. The treated timber surface should be left unsealed for at least two weeks to allow the insecticide to penetrate to the deeper tunnels.
Confirming eradication

Adult furniture beetles emerge between May and August. Repeat the frass test the following spring by placing fresh paper under the piece and checking for new powder. No fresh frass means the treatment was successful. New frass means surviving larvae have pupated and a second treatment is needed. Most infestations clear with one or two treatments; persistent re-infestation in structural timbers may indicate a source piece in the same room that has not been treated.
Common mistakes
Treatment applied over a polished or varnished surface.

The active ingredient cannot penetrate a sealed surface. Strip any finish from areas being treated, or inject the fluid directly into the exit holes using a syringe nozzle, before brushing the bare timber.
Treatment applied to old, inactive holes.

Old holes with dark frass or no frass do not indicate a live infestation. Confirm activity with the paper frass test before treating; unnecessary treatment is wasted product and unnecessary chemical exposure.
